Numerical Linear Algebra

Matrix Multiplication, Factorization, and Solving Linear Systems

Matrix Multiplication, Factorization, and Solving Linear Systems

In the previous chapter we treated AxAxAx as a linear combination of the columns of AAA. That single idea is the seed from which almost everything in this chapter grows. We first extend it from matrix times vector to matrix times matrix, viewing the result in two complementary ways — as a collection of inner products and as a sum of outer products. That second view turns out to be more than a curiosity: it hands us the key insight behind our first true matrix factorization, the CR factorization, which expresses any matrix in terms of its independent columns. From there we introduce the LU factorization, a tool purpose-built for solving Ax=bAx = bAx=b efficiently, and close by formalizing what it means for a matrix to be invertible and how that concept relates back to solving linear systems in practice.

Matrix–Vector Multiplication

We start by revisiting matrix–vector multiplication, this time paying close attention to how the output is built rather than just what it equals.

Matrix–Vector Multiplication

Given a matrix AAA and a vector x=[v1v2]x = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{bmatrix}x=[v1​v2​​], the product AxAxAx is computed row by row:

Ax=[2537][v1v2]=[2v1+5v23v1+7v2]Ax = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 7 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2v_1 + 5v_2 \\ 3v_1 + 7v_2 \end{bmatrix}Ax=[23​57​][v1​v2​​]=[2v1​+5v2​3v1​+7v2​​]

For example, setting v1=v2=1v_1 = v_2 = 1v1​=v2​=1 gives Ax=[710]Ax = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}Ax=[710​].

This row-by-row computation is correct, but it obscures a more useful way of thinking about the product. If instead we group the terms by which column of AAA they came from, we see that AxAxAx is nothing more than a weighted sum of AAA‘s columns, with the entries of xxx acting as the weights.

Matrix–Vector Multiplication as a Linear Combination

The same product can be written as a linear combination of the columns of AAA:

Ax=[2537][v1v2]=v1[23]+v2[57]Ax = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 7 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{bmatrix} = v_1 \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} + v_2 \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}Ax=[23​57​][v1​v2​​]=v1​[23​]+v2​[57​]

With v1=v2=1v_1 = v_2 = 1v1​=v2​=1, this again gives [710]\begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}[710​], confirming the two views agree.

This “linear combination of columns” perspective is the one we will lean on for the rest of the chapter, because it generalizes cleanly once we move from multiplying a matrix by a single vector to multiplying two matrices together.

Matrix–Matrix Multiplication

A matrix–matrix product C=ABC = ABC=AB can be understood as many matrix–vector products stacked side by side: each column of CCC is AAA times the corresponding column of BBB. Written out entry by entry, this gives the familiar formula for each entry of CCC.

Matrix–Matrix Multiplication

Given matrices A=[ai,k]A = [a_{i,k}]A=[ai,k​] and B=[bk,j]B = [b_{k,j}]B=[bk,j​], their product C=AB=[ci,j]C = AB = [c_{i,j}]C=AB=[ci,j​] has entries

ci,j=∑kai,k bk,jc_{i,j} = \sum_k a_{i,k}\,b_{k,j}ci,j​=k∑​ai,k​bk,j​

Each entry ci,jc_{i,j}ci,j​ is the dot product of row iii of AAA with column jjj of BBB. This pattern continues across every entry of CCC: to fill in position (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), pair row iii of the left matrix with column jjj of the right matrix and sum the products.

This entrywise formula is really just the dot product in disguise, since each entry of CCC is computed by taking the dot product of a row of AAA with a column of BBB. It is worth seeing this play out with concrete numbers before moving to the more conceptual outer-product view.

Example: Inner Product View (Matrix × Matrix → Matrix)
[1234][5768]=[(1)(5)+(2)(6)(1)(7)+(2)(8)(3)(5)+(4)(6)(3)(7)+(4)(8)]=[17233953]\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \\ 6 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(5) + (2)(6) & (1)(7) + (2)(8) \\ (3)(5) + (4)(6) & (3)(7) + (4)(8) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 17 & 23 \\ 39 & 53 \end{bmatrix}[13​24​][56​78​]=[(1)(5)+(2)(6)(3)(5)+(4)(6)​(1)(7)+(2)(8)(3)(7)+(4)(8)​]=[1739​2353​]

Row-times-column is the standard way to compute a matrix product by hand, but it isn’t the only way to think about it — and it isn’t the most illuminating one for our purposes. There is a second, equally valid way to build the same product: instead of combining rows of AAA with columns of BBB, we can combine columns of AAA with rows of BBB, one pair at a time, and add up the results. Each such pairing is an outer product, and it produces a full matrix rather than a scalar.

Example: Outer Product View (Vector × Vector → Matrix)

Take the first column of AAA, [13]\begin{bmatrix}1\\3\end{bmatrix}[13​], and pair it with the first row of BBB, [57]\begin{bmatrix}5 & 7\end{bmatrix}[5​7​]:

[13][57]=[571521]=C1\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \\ 15 & 21 \end{bmatrix} = C_1[13​][5​7​]=[515​721​]=C1​

Now take the second column of AAA, [24]\begin{bmatrix}2\\4\end{bmatrix}[24​], and pair it with the second row of BBB, [68]\begin{bmatrix}6 & 8\end{bmatrix}[6​8​]:

[24][68]=[12162432]=C2\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 16 \\ 24 & 32 \end{bmatrix} = C_2[24​][6​8​]=[1224​1632​]=C2​

Adding the two outer products together recovers exactly the product we computed above:

[571521]+[12162432]=[17233953]=C1+C2=C\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \\ 15 & 21 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 16 \\ 24 & 32 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 17 & 23 \\ 39 & 53 \end{bmatrix} = C_1 + C_2 = C[515​721​]+[1224​1632​]=[1739​2353​]=C1​+C2​=C
Corollary

C1C_1C1​ and C2C_2C2​ are each rank-one matrices, since each is built as the outer product of a single pair of vectors. This illustrates a general fact: any matrix product can be decomposed into a sum of rank-one pieces, one per shared dimension between the two factors.

The outer-product view is more than a computational trick — it reframes multiplication itself as a sum of simple, rank-one building blocks. That reframing is exactly what we need to start thinking about factoring a matrix, which is the subject of the rest of this chapter.

Matrix Factorization Methods

All of the factorization methods introduced in this course exist for a single practical purpose: solving linear systems. Given a matrix AAA and vector bbb, we want to find xxx satisfying

Ax=b.Ax = b.Ax=b.

Rather than attacking this equation head-on, we will repeatedly break AAA apart into a product of simpler matrices — triangular, orthogonal, or diagonal — each of which makes solving the system easier. The major factorizations we will encounter are:

A=CRA=LUA=QRS=QΛQTA=UΣVT\begin{align} & A=CR \\ & A=LU \\ & A=QR \\ & S=Q\Lambda Q^T \\ & A = U \Sigma V^T \end{align}​A=CRA=LUA=QRS=QΛQTA=UΣVT​​

We begin with the simplest of these, the CR factorization, which follows directly from the outer-product idea above.

CR Factorization

The outer-product decomposition showed that a matrix can be rebuilt from a sum of rank-one pieces. The CR factorization takes this one step further: rather than summing over every column, it isolates just the independent columns of AAA and expresses every other column as a combination of them.

CR Factorization

Goal: Given a matrix AAA, factor it as

A=CRA = CRA=CR

Process:

  1. Let CCC contain the first rrr linearly independent columns of AAA, where r=rank(A)r = \text{rank}(A)r=rank(A).
  2. Choose RRR so that column jjj of A=CRA = CRA=CR reproduces column jjj of AAA as the corresponding linear combination of the columns of CCC.

Working through a concrete example makes the process concrete: we identify which columns of AAA are independent, place them in CCC, and record the combinations needed to rebuild the remaining columns in RRR.

Example 1
A=[124135]=[a1⃗a2⃗a3⃗]A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \vec{a_1} & \vec{a_2} & \vec{a_3} \end{bmatrix}A=[11​23​45​]=[a1​​​a2​​​a3​​​]

Observe the linear dependence of a3⃗\vec{a_3}a3​​:

a3⃗=2a1⃗+a2⃗\vec{a_3} = 2\vec{a_1} + \vec{a_2}a3​​=2a1​​+a2​​

There is no combination of a1⃗\vec{a_1}a1​​ and a2⃗\vec{a_2}a2​​ that produces the other, so these two columns are linearly independent — they form CCC. The matrix RRR then records how every column of AAA (including a1⃗\vec{a_1}a1​​ and a2⃗\vec{a_2}a2​​ themselves) is built from CCC:

[124135]⏟A=[1213]⏟C[102011]⏟R\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix}}_{A} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}}_{C} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{R}A[11​23​45​]​​=C[11​23​]​​R[10​01​21​]​​

The example above involved a matrix of full column rank among a⃗1,a⃗2\vec a_1, \vec a_2a1​,a2​. It’s also worth seeing what happens at the opposite extreme, where a matrix has rank as low as possible: rank one. This connects back to the corollary from the outer-product example.

Linking Rank and Products
  1. Every rank-one matrix can be written as the outer product of exactly one pair of vectors (up to scaling).
  2. Every rank-rrr matrix can be written as the sum of rrr rank-one matrices.
Example 2
A=[246369]A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}A=[23​46​69​]

Here every column is a multiple of [23]\begin{bmatrix}2\\3\end{bmatrix}[23​], so rank(A)=1\text{rank}(A) = 1rank(A)=1 and CCC consists of that single column. Writing each column of AAA as a multiple of CCC gives R=[123]R = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 2 & 3\end{bmatrix}R=[1​2​3​], and the CR factorization collapses to the single outer product

A=[23][123]A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}A=[23​][1​2​3​]

consistent with the corollary above: a rank-one matrix is exactly one outer product.

LU Factorization

CR factorization organizes a matrix around its independent columns, which is useful for understanding structure but is not the most efficient route to solving Ax=bAx = bAx=b when AAA is square. For that purpose we turn to the LU factorization, which splits AAA into a lower triangular and an upper triangular matrix — two forms that are each easy to solve against.

LU Factorization

Consider a square matrix AAA factored as A=LUA = LUA=LU. The system Ax=bAx = bAx=b becomes

Ax=b≡L(Ux)=b\begin{align} Ax & = b \\ & \equiv \\ L(Ux) & = b \end{align}AxL(Ux)​=b≡=b​​

Process:

  1. Solve Lc=bLc = bLc=b for ccc (forward substitution, since LLL is lower triangular).

  2. Solve Ux=cUx = cUx=c for xxx (back substitution, since UUU is upper triangular).

Note: Cost of Computation
  • Computing the LULULU factorization itself costs O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3), where nnn is the number of rows (equivalently, columns) of AAA.
  • Once LLL and UUU are known, back-substitution costs only O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2).
  • This asymmetry makes LULULU especially attractive when we need to solve Ax=bAx = bAx=b for many different right-hand sides bbb but the same matrix AAA: the expensive O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3) factorization is done once, and each new solve costs only O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2).

Solving Linear Systems

With CR and LU in hand, we can now return to the original question that motivates this whole chapter — solving Ax=bAx = bAx=b — and ask when a unique solution is guaranteed to exist. The answer hinges on invertibility.

Invertible Matrices

Invertible Matrix Theorem

A square matrix AAA is invertible if and only if any (equivalently, all) of the following hold:

  • AAA has no zero eigenvalues.
  • The only solution to Ax=0Ax = 0Ax=0 is the trivial solution x=0x = 0x=0.
  • rank(A)=(number of columns)=(number of rows)\text{rank}(A) = \text{(number of columns)} = \text{(number of rows)}rank(A)=(number of columns)=(number of rows).

These conditions are different ways of saying the same thing: an invertible matrix wastes none of its rows or columns on redundant information, so no nonzero vector is ever mapped to zero. When this holds, we can formally solve Ax=bAx = bAx=b by introducing the inverse A−1A^{-1}A−1, defined by AA−1=A−1A=IAA^{-1} = A^{-1}A = IAA−1=A−1A=I.

Invertible Matrices and Solving Ax=bAx = bAx=b

Suppose AAA is a square, invertible matrix, so that AB=BA=IAB = BA = IAB=BA=I for some matrix B=A−1B = A^{-1}B=A−1. We can solve Ax=bAx = bAx=b as follows:

  1. Start with Ax=bAx = bAx=b.
  2. Multiply both sides on the left by A−1A^{-1}A−1.
  3. This gives:
A−1Ax=A−1b  ⟹  x=A−1b.A^{-1}Ax = A^{-1}b \;\Longrightarrow\; x = A^{-1}b.A−1Ax=A−1b⟹x=A−1b.

This identity defines the inverse and shows that the solution xxx is unique whenever A−1A^{-1}A−1 exists.

It’s tempting to treat x=A−1bx = A^{-1}bx=A−1b as a recipe for computing solutions directly — form A−1A^{-1}A−1, then multiply. In practice, this is almost always the wrong approach.

Important Computational Note

Avoid forming A−1A^{-1}A−1 explicitly in numerical computations. Solve Ax=bAx = bAx=b instead via a factorization, such as LULULU or QRQRQR. This approach is typically faster and substantially more numerically stable than explicitly computing and applying an inverse.

This closing point is what ties the whole chapter together: the factorizations we developed above — CR to expose structure, LU to solve efficiently — are not academic exercises. They are the practical alternative to the inverse, and the standard tools by which real linear systems are actually solved.
